meta.name: PSWQ Penn State Worry Questionnaire
meta.description: The PSWQ is a 16-item scale measuring trait worry, a core feature of generalized anxiety disorder.
meta.author: Meyer TJ, et al. (1990)
meta.reference: Meyer TJ, et al. Development and validation of the Penn State Worry Questionnaire. Behav Res Ther. 1990;28(6):487-495.
meta.instruction: Rate each of the following statements on a scale that best describes how typical each is of you.

commonChoices.label: Not at all typical of me

questions.text: If I do not have enough time to do everything, I do not worry about it.
I do not tend to worry about things.
I know I should not worry about things, but I just cannot help it.
When I am under pressure I worry a lot.
I am always worrying about something.
I find it easy to dismiss worrisome thoughts.
As soon as I finish one task, I start to worry about everything else I have to do.
When there is nothing more I can do about a concern, I do not worry about it any more.
I have been a worrier all my life.
I notice that I have been worrying about things.
Once I start worrying, I cannot stop.
I worry about projects until they are all done.

ranges.label: Low Worry
ranges.description: Your score suggests low levels of pathological worry. You appear to manage uncertainty and everyday concerns without excessive rumination. Some worry is a normal and adaptive response to life challenges.
suggestions: Continue using your current effective coping strategies
Maintain activities and relationships that help manage everyday stress

ranges.label: Moderate Worry
ranges.description: Your score indicates moderate levels of worry that may be affecting your daily comfort and functioning. You may find it difficult to control worry, experience worry that seems disproportionate to the situation, or notice that worrying interferes with concentration or sleep.
suggestions: Practice worry management techniques such as setting aside a specific 'worry time'
Learn to distinguish between productive problem-solving and unproductive rumination
Regular exercise and mindfulness meditation can help reduce chronic worry
Consider consulting a mental health professional if worry is significantly affecting your daily life

ranges.label: Severe Pathological Worry
ranges.description: Your score suggests severe pathological worry consistent with generalized anxiety disorder (GAD). At this level, worry is likely pervasive, difficult to control, and significantly interfering with your work, relationships, sleep, and overall well-being. Evidence-based treatment can be very effective.
suggestions: Seek evaluation from a mental health professional experienced in anxiety disorders
Cognitive behavioral therapy (CBT) is the first-line treatment for GAD and chronic worry
Medication (such as SSRIs or SNRIs) may also be helpful, especially in combination with therapy
Practice grounding and relaxation techniques for acute episodes of worry
GAD is very treatable; most people improve significantly with appropriate help
